title: Children of God and Love in Action
subtitle: What we shall be, practicing righteousness, loving in deed and truth

sections.header: Verses 1-10 - "Children of God and Children of the Devil"

heb.paragraph: See what kind of love the Father has given us, that we should be called children of God - the designation is not metaphor but reality.
Everyone who thus hopes in him purifies himself as he is pure - hope motivates holiness. Future transformation shapes present pursuit.
Everyone who makes a practice of sinning practices lawlessness; sin is lawlessness - sin is rebellion against divine order.

refs.note: To all who received him, he gave the right to become children of God.
Conformed to the image of his Son.
He has appeared once for all to put away sin.
Born again of imperishable seed through the word of God.

notes.note: See what love - that we should be called children of God! When he appears we shall be like him. Everyone hoping in him purifies himself.
Sin is lawlessness. He appeared to take away sins; in him is no sin. No one abiding in him keeps on sinning.
Whoever practices righteousness is righteous. Whoever practices sin is of the devil. No one born of God makes a practice of sinning.
Calvin emphasizes the already/not-yet: we are children now, but full transformation awaits. Hope purifies by directing desire toward glory.
John speaks of habitual practice, not isolated acts. The present tense indicates ongoing pattern. Children of God do not persist in sin.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: When he appears - either God or Christ. Context slightly favors Christ, given the parallel with 2:28.
God seed (sperma autou) - either the divine nature implanted in believers or the word of God. Both interpretations have support.

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough emphasizes that John is not teaching sinless perfection but distinguishing patterns of life. The direction of life reveals parentage.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse notes the stark contrast: children of God vs. children of the devil. There is no middle category. Lifestyle reveals lineage.

hist.historical: First John addresses a crisis caused by secessionists who have left the Johannine community (2:19). These opponents likely held proto-Gnostic views: denying that Jesus Christ came 'in the flesh' (4:2), claiming sinless perfection while living immorally (1:8-10), and failing to love fellow believers (2:9-11). The author, traditionally identified as the apostle John writing from Ephesus in the 90s AD, establishes tests of authentic Christianity: right belief about Christ's incarnation, righteous living, and love for the community. The letter's repetitive, spiral structure reinforces these themes through variation rather than linear argument. The community had experienced a traumatic split, and the letter reassures those who remained of their standing with God.
hist.context: See what kind of love the Father has given us, that we should be called children of God - and so we are! The world does not know us because it did not know him. Beloved, we are God children now, and what we will be has not yet appeared; but when he appears we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is. Everyone who hopes in him purifies himself. Everyone who makes a practice of sinning practices lawlessness. You know that he appeared to take away sins, and in him there is no sin. No one who abides in him keeps on sinning; no one who keeps on sinning has either seen him or known him. Little children, let no one deceive you. Whoever practices righteousness is righteous. Whoever makes a practice of sinning is of the devil. The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the works of the devil. No one born of God makes a practice of sinning, for God seed abides in him. By this it is evident who are the children of God and children of the devil.

heb.paragraph: We have passed out of death into life because we love the brothers - love is evidence of spiritual resurrection.
Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer - hatred is incipient murder. The heart condition matters, not just the action.
If anyone has the world goods and sees his brother in need yet closes his heart - practical love requires open hands.

refs.note: Cain rose up against his brother Abel and killed him.
This is my commandment, that you love one another. Greater love has no one than this.
Everyone who is angry with his brother will be liable to judgment.
If a brother or sister is poorly clothed and lacking daily food...

notes.note: Love one another from the beginning. Not like Cain who was of the evil one and murdered his righteous brother.
Do not be surprised at world hatred. We have passed from death to life because we love. Whoever hates is a murderer.
He laid down his life; we ought to lay down ours. If anyone has goods and sees need but closes his heart - how does God love abide? Love in deed, not just word.
Calvin emphasizes that hatred and murder are on the same continuum. The heart attitude is what God evaluates, not just external action.
Christ sacrifice is the standard of love. Compared to his death, sharing possessions is minimal. Yet even this we often fail.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Passed out of death into life (metabebekamen) - perfect tense indicates accomplished transition with ongoing results.
Closes his heart (kleise ta splanchna) - literally shuts his bowels. Ancient psychology located emotions in the intestines.

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough emphasizes that love is not sentiment but action. Verse 18 summarizes: deed and truth, not word and talk.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse notes the progression: Cain (negative example) to Christ (positive example) to us (our response). Love requires sacrifice.

hist.context: For this is the message you have heard from the beginning: that we should love one another. We should not be like Cain, who was of the evil one and murdered his brother. And why did he murder him? Because his own deeds were evil and his brother deeds were righteous. Do not be surprised, brothers, that the world hates you. We know that we have passed out of death into life because we love the brothers. Whoever does not love abides in death. Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer, and you know that no murderer has eternal life abiding in him. By this we know love, that he laid down his life for us, and we ought to lay down our lives for the brothers. But if anyone has the world goods and sees his brother in need, yet closes his heart against him, how does God love abide in him? Little children, let us not love in word or talk but in deed and in truth.

heb.paragraph: Whenever our heart condemns us, God is greater than our heart - God knowledge exceeds our self-awareness. He sees what we miss.
If our heart does not condemn us, we have confidence before God - clear conscience enables bold approach.
The Spirit whom he has given us - the Spirit internal witness confirms our relationship with God.

refs.note: There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.
Whatever you ask in my name, this I will do.
This is the work of God, that you believe in him whom he has sent.
The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God.

notes.note: By this we know we are of the truth. When our heart condemns us, God is greater than our heart - he knows everything.
If our heart does not condemn us, we have confidence. Whatever we ask we receive because we keep his commandments.
His commandment: believe in his Son Jesus Christ and love one another. Whoever keeps his commandments abides in God. The Spirit confirms this.
Calvin interprets God greater than our heart as assurance: even when conscience accuses, God grace is greater. He knows our sincere faith.
The double commandment - believe and love - summarizes the whole. Faith and love are inseparable responses to God.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: God is greater - this could be assurance (he knows our faith despite our failures) or warning (he knows even more than our conscience accuses). Context favors assurance.
The Spirit confirms mutual abiding. This is the first explicit mention of the Spirit since 2:27 (the anointing).

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough emphasizes the role of conscience, transformed by gospel and confirmed by Spirit. Assurance is not presumption but grounded confidence.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse notes the summary of commandments in v. 23: Christological faith and brotherly love. These are the twin marks of authentic Christianity.

hist.context: By this we shall know that we are of the truth and reassure our heart before him; for whenever our heart condemns us, God is greater than our heart, and he knows everything. Beloved, if our heart does not condemn us, we have confidence before God; and whatever we ask we receive from him, because we keep his commandments and do what pleases him. And this is his commandment, that we believe in the name of his Son Jesus Christ and love one another, just as he has commanded us. Whoever keeps his commandments abides in God, and God in him. And by this we know that he abides in us, by the Spirit whom he has given us.

themes.note: Faith and holiness dominate as the marks of God children. Mercy appears in Christ laying down his life. Justice underlies the distinction between two families.

rows.label: vv. 1-10
rows.text: Children of God vs. children of devil

rows.label: vv. 11-18
rows.text: Love in deed and truth; Cain and Christ

lit.note: Chapter 3 distinguishes children of God from children of the devil by their practice of righteousness and love. Confidence before God rests on obedience and Spirit witness.

discourse.title: 1 John 3 — Children of God and Brotherly Love
discourse.thesis: We are God's children now; what we will be has not yet appeared. Sin is incompatible with being born of God. Love in deed, not just word.

nodes.label: What manner of love (3:1-3)
nodes.text: See what kind of love the Father has given us, that we should be called children of God — and so we are! The world does not know us because it did not know him. When he appears, we shall be like him, for we shall see him as he is. Everyone who hopes in him purifies himself.

nodes.label: Sin and the devil (3:4-10)
nodes.text: Everyone who makes a practice of sinning is of the devil, for the devil has been sinning from the beginning. The Son of God appeared to destroy the devil's works. No one born of God makes a practice of sinning — God's seed abides in him. By this it is evident who are children of God and children of the devil.

nodes.label: Love one another (3:11-18)
nodes.text: This is the message from the beginning: love one another. Not like Cain who murdered his brother. Do not be surprised if the world hates you. We have passed from death to life because we love the brothers. Whoever does not love abides in death. By this we know love: he laid down his life for us. We ought to lay down our lives for the brothers. If anyone has the world's goods and sees his brother in need but closes his heart, how does God's love abide in him? Let us love in deed and truth, not in word or talk.

nodes.label: Confidence before God (3:19-24)
nodes.text: By this we know we are of the truth and reassure our hearts. If our hearts condemn us, God is greater than our hearts. If our hearts do not condemn us, we have confidence before God. This is his commandment: believe in Jesus and love one another. Whoever keeps his commandments abides in God, and we know he abides in us by the Spirit.

discourse.note: The 'seed' of God (3:9) introduces the idea of transformation so fundamental that habitual sin becomes incongruous.
